planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring through early fall in well-drained, slightly acidic soil; glossy abelia is one of the workhorses of Southern landscaping, adaptable to full sun or partial shade and beloved for its long season of small, fragrant white flowers attractive to hummingbirds and butterflies.

directions: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or early fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; adaptable to a range of soil types but dislikes waterlogging. Glossy Abelia is a hybrid shrub (A. chinensis x A. uniflora) that has been a cornerstone of Southern gardens since its introduction in the 1800s; it produces arching branches with small, glossy, semi-evergreen to evergreen leaves that turn attractive reddish-bronze in fall, and from early summer through frost bears masses of small, tubular, white-to-pale-pink, delicately fragrant flowers that are among the longest-blooming of any shrub and are magnets for hummingbirds, bumblebees, and butterflies including cloudless sulphur. Prune to shape in late winter before new growth; it blooms on new wood so pruning does not eliminate flowers. In zone 6–7 it may die back to the ground and resprout vigorously each spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets or container plants in spring in any well-drained soil in full to partial sun; among the most adaptable and Gulf Coast-appropriate yuccas, with soft, flexible leaf tips that do not injure passersby and a refined appearance suitable for close-quarters landscape use.

directions: Plant offsets or container-grown plants in spring in any well-drained to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants are extremely drought-tolerant and require virtually no maintenance once established. Yucca recurvifolia is among the most garden-friendly of all yuccas because its long, blue-green leaves recurve or droop gracefully at the tips rather than ending in a sharp point, making it safe to plant along walkways and in high-traffic areas; it produces tall (3–5 ft) spikes of large, creamy white, bell-shaped flowers in early summer that are pollinated by yucca moths and are dramatic and beautiful. The plant spreads slowly by offsets to form attractive colonies and is extremely tolerant of heat, drought, salt spray, and poor sandy soils — a nearly indestructible landscape plant for Gulf Coast and zone 8–11 gardens.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Chihuahuan sage is the most compact and finely textured of the Leucophyllum species and tends to have a naturally rounded habit that rarely needs pruning — ideal for formal low hedges in zone 8–11 landscapes.

directions: Plant in spring or early fall in extremely well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Leucophyllum species suffer in waterlogged or heavily amended acidic soils and must have excellent drainage to thrive. Chihuahuan sage (L. laevigatum) is the most compact and finely textured of the native Texas sage species, with smooth, small, grey-green leaves and a naturally mounded, nearly spherical habit that makes it one of the most refined-looking drought-tolerant shrubs for formal and informal use in hot, dry landscapes; like all Leucophyllum, it responds to humidity and soil moisture by bursting into bloom with masses of small, lavender to purple tubular flowers — a phenomenon known as 'barometer bush' behavior because blooming is triggered by summer monsoon rains. Essentially maintenance-free once established and highly tolerant of alkaline caliche soils.
sources: Arizona State University Desert Plants Collection

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or early fall in moist to dry, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; autumn moor grass is one of the few ornamental grasses that genuinely performs well in dry shade — a rare and valuable quality for challenging landscape situations.

directions: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist to dry, well-drained soil; autumn moor grass establishes readily and begins producing attractive flower spikes in its first season. Sesleria autumnalis is a compact, fine-textured European moor grass that is one of the most valuable ornamental grasses for difficult garden situations: it is genuinely shade-tolerant, performing well in both partial and dappled shade, it is drought-tolerant once established, and it blooms in late summer and fall when most ornamental grasses are already looking tired — the chartreuse to cream, bottlebrush-like flower spikes emerge in August through October above bright green, arching foliage that stays attractive nearly year-round. The low, mounding habit (12–18 inches) makes it ideal for edging, mass planting under trees, or mixing with shade perennials such as hostas and heucheras.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Yeddo hawthorn is slower-growing and more compact than Indian hawthorn (R. indica) with larger, thicker, darker leaves and larger white flowers — a more refined species for formal use.

directions: Plant in spring or early fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Yeddo hawthorn is drought-tolerant once established and extremely tolerant of salt spray and coastal conditions, making it an outstanding choice for beachfront and coastal zone 8–10 landscapes. Yeddo hawthorn (R. umbellata) is the Japanese species of Raphiolepis, larger and slower-growing than the commonly sold Indian hawthorn (R. indica), with distinctly thick, leathery, very dark green rounded leaves and showy clusters of large, pure white fragrant flowers in spring followed by dark blue-black berries attractive to birds; the overall appearance is more refined and dramatic than other Raphiolepis. Excellent as a specimen plant, in foundation plantings, or as a formal hedge in coastal and Gulf Coast landscapes where salt tolerance is essential.
